title: Linear response from tilted Dirac cones under strain-induced pseudomagnetic fields
abstract: We investigate the transport signatures of pseudo-Landau levels (PLLs) in two-dimensional anisotropic Dirac systems with tilted cones, whose effective bandstructure results from strain-induced pseudogauge fields. In contrast to conventional Landau quantisation, the PLLs exhibit explicit momentum-dependence by being dispersive, leading to finite longitudinal group-velocities. We analyse the transport properties within the semiclassical Boltzmann framework by computing the electrical, thermoelectric, and thermal response in the linear regime, which acquire nonzero longitudinal components. We also check the validity of the Mott relation and Wiedemann-Franz law in our system. Our results provide a unified framework for understanding the interplay between tilted spectrum and structural deformation in affecting quantum transport, and suggest unambiguous experimental signatures in strain-engineered systems.
summary: This paper explores how mechanical strain and the resulting pseudomagnetic fields modify the electronic properties of 2D Dirac materials. Unlike standard Landau levels, the levels in these strained, tilted systems are dispersive, meaning they depend on momentum. This dispersion allows for finite longitudinal transport that can be controlled by tuning the tilt and strain. The study provides a theoretical framework for using mechanical deformation to engineer conductance in valleytronic devices.
main_problem: Investigating how the interplay between tilted Dirac cones and strain-induced pseudomagnetic fields affects the dispersion and longitudinal transport properties of 2D anisotropic Dirac systems.
main_result: The authors show that pseudo-Landau levels (PLLs) are dispersive due to the tilt, leading to finite longitudinal group velocities and non-vanishing transport coefficients, which can be tuned via strain.
method: The study uses a semiclassical Boltzmann framework and solves the eigenvalue equation for the Hamiltonian using a Sturm-Liouville approach and confluent hypergeometric functions.
model_or_system: A 2D anisotropic Dirac system (such as graphene or organic conductors) subject to uniaxial strain, modeled by a generalized Weyl-like Hamiltonian with a tilt parameter and a strain-induced pseudogauge field.
key_observables: Longitudinal electrical conductivity, thermoelectric conductivity, and thermal conductivity, as well as the validity of the Mott relation and Wiedemann-Franz law.
important_parameters: Tilt parameter (eta_x), pseudomagnetic field strength (B), chemical potential (mu), temperature (T), and velocity anisotropy (v_y/v_x).
main_assumptions: Assumes a type-I (undertilted) phase, small strain magnitude, and that the strain primarily affects off-diagonal hopping terms.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows tilted energy bands and an experimental setup schematic; Figure 2 displays the dispersion of pseudo-Landau levels; Figure 3 plots longitudinal response coefficients as functions of chemical potential.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the tilted Hamiltonian and strain mechanism, derives the dispersive pseudo-Landau level spectrum and wavefunctions, applies the Boltzmann formalism to calculate transport coefficients, and concludes by testing the validity of standard transport relations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Energy bands for a tilted Dirac cone. (b) Set-up to measure the longitudinal transport coefficients in the presence of a strain-induced pseudomagnetic field. Here, a uniform and time-independent electric field (Ex) or temperature gradient (−∂xT) is applied in the x-direction and Vx is the associated voltage.
FIG. 2. Dispersion of the PLLs for some chosen parameters shown in the plot-labels.
FIG. 3. Linear-response coefficients ˜σxx, ˜αxx, and ˜ℓxx, as functions of ˜µ. We have plotted them for 3 different values of β. We have taken vy/vx = 0.8.
FIG. 4. Variation of electrical conductivity with ηx by setting vy/vx to 0.8. Here, δσ ≡σxx(ηx)/σxx(ηx = 0) −1.
FIG. 5. Variation of electrical conductivity with B.

FIG. 6. Comparision of the Mott Relation and the Wiedemann-Franz (WF) law [cf. Eq. (15)] with the results obtained from the original integrals [cf. Eq. (13)]. While the blue lines represent the results obtained from the actual integrals (indicated by the label “Integral”), the dotted red lines show the answers obtained by using the relations. We have used here vy/vx = 0.8.
